Title: Elicit - Detecting Overfitting in Algorithmic Trading Strat - Report.pdf
COMPUTING THE PROBABILITY OF OVERFITTING IN THE BACKTESTING AND OPTIMIZATION OF INVESTMENT STRATEGIES
Summary: Most firms and portfolio managers rely on backtests (or historical simulations of performance) to select investment strategies and allocate them capital. We prove that after trying only 7 strategy configurations, a researcher is expected to identify at least one 2-year long backtest with an annualized Sharpe ratio of over 1, when the expected out of sample Sharpe ratio is 0. If the researcher tries a large enough number of strategy configurations, a backtest can always be fit to any desired performance for a fixed sample length. We find that there is a minimum backtest length (MinBTL) that should be required for a given number of trials. A Sharpe ratio of 10 over 50 years requires more overfitting effort than a Sharpe ratio of 2 over 5 years, but nowadays’ computational power makes backtest overfitting a relatively easy task. The practical totality of published backtests do not report the number of trials involved, thus we suspect that many backtests may be substantially overfit. Standard statistical techniques designed to prevent regression overfitting, such as holdout, tend to be unreliable and inaccurate in the context of investment backtests. We propose a framework that estimates the probability of backtest overfitting (PBO) specifically in the context of investment simulations, through a numerical method that we call combinatorially symmetric cross-validation (CSCV). We show that CSCV produces accurate estimates of the probability that a particular backtest is overfit.

Title: The Deflated Sharpe Ratio: Correcting for Selection Bias, Backtest Overfitting, and Non-Normality
Authors: David H. Bailey, Marcos M. López de Prado
Summary: With the advent in recent years of large financial data sets, machine learning and high-performance computing, analysts can backtest millions (if not billions) of alternative investment strategies. Backtest optimizers search for combinations of parameters that maximize the simulated historical performance of a strategy, leading to backtest overfitting. The problem of performance inflation extends beyond backtesting. More generally, researchers and investors tend to report only positive outcomes, a phenomenon known as selection bias. Not controlling for the number of trials involved in a particular discovery leads to over-optimistic performance expectations. The Deflated Sharpe Ratio (DSR) corrects for two leading sources of performance inflation: Selection bias under multiple testing and non-Normally distributed returns. In doing so, DSR helps separate legitimate empirical findings from statistical flukes.

Title: All That Glitters Is Not Gold: Comparing Backtest and Out-of-Sample Performance on a Large Cohort of Trading Algorithms
Authors: Thomas V. Wiecki, Andrew Campbell, J. Lent, Jessica Stauth
Summary: When automated trading strategies are developed and evaluated using backtests on historical pricing data, there exists a tendency to overfit to the past. Using a unique dataset of 888 algorithmic trading strategies developed and backtested on the Quantopian platform, with at least six months of out-of-sample performance, this article studies the prevalence and impact of backtest overfitting. Specifically, the authors find that commonly reported backtest evaluation metrics, such as the Sharpe ratio, offer little value in predicting out-of-sample performance (R 2 < 0.025). In contrast, higher-order moments, such as volatility and maximum drawdown, as well as portfolio construction features (e.g., hedging), show significant predictive value of relevance to quantitative finance practitioners. Moreover, in line with prior theoretical considerations, the authors find empirical evidence of overfitting—the more backtesting a quant has done for a strategy, the larger the discrepancy between backtest and out-of-sample performance. Finally, they show that by training nonlinear, machine-learning classifiers on a variety of features that describe backtest behavior, out-of-sample performance can be predicted with much greater accuracy (R 2 = 0.17) on hold-out data than when using linear, univariate features. A portfolio constructed by using predictions on hold-out data performed significantly better out-of-sample than one constructed from algorithms with the highest backtest Sharpe ratios.

Title: A comparison of machine learning model validation schemes for non-stationary time series data
Summary: Machine learning is increasingly applied to time series data, as it constitutes an attractive alternative to forecasts based on traditional time series models. For independent and identically distributed observations, cross-validation is the prevalent scheme for estimating out-of-sample performance in both model selection and assessment. For time series data, however, it is unclear whether forwardvalidation schemes, i.e., schemes that keep the temporal order of observations, should be preferred. In this paper, we perform a comprehensive empirical study of eight common validation schemes. We introduce a study design that perturbs global stationarity by introducing a slow evolution of the underlying data-generating process. Our results demonstrate that, even for relatively small perturbations, commonly used cross-validation schemes often yield estimates with the largest bias and variance, and forward-validation schemes yield better estimates of the out-of-sample error. We provide an interpretation of these results in terms of an additional evolution-induced bias and the sample-size dependent estimation error. Using a large-scale financial data set, we demonstrate the practical significance in a replication study of a statistical arbitrage problem. We conclude with some general guidelines on the selection of suitable validation schemes for time series data.

###### Financial Risk Manager (FRM®)

# FRM Certification

Join the 97,000+ professionals from over 190 countries and regions who have completed their FRM Certification and earned financial risk management’s mark of excellence.

## About the FRM Program

As the true standard for educational excellence in risk management, GARP’s FRM Certification is proof that you’re ready and able to assess, measure, and monitor risk in real-world situations, placing you in a strong position to improve your job performance and advance your career.

FRM Program and Exams

### Request More Information

Sign up to receive our comprehensive FRM Candidate Guide. It includes all the information needed to earn the FRM Certification and open new career pathways with risk expertise.

## Get to Know the FRM Program

Learn more about the globally recognized standard for financial risk management.

Certification Process

---

### How can I earn the FRM Certification?

To earn your Certification, you’ll need to pass two multiple-choice Exams and submit evidence of at least two years of relevant work experience. Once you become certified, you are strongly encouraged to participate in GARP’s Continuing Professional Development (CPD) program to help stay up-to-date with the latest best practices in risk management.

Exam Structure

---

### What is the structure of the FRM Exam?

The FRM Exam Part I consists of 100 multiple-choice questions focusing on risk management foundations, quantitative analysis, financial markets and products, and valuation and risk models. The FRM Exam Part II comprises 80 questions emphasizing the application of the tools and techniques covered in Part I; topics include market risk, credit risk, operational risk and resilience, liquidity and treasury risk, risk management and investment management, and current financial market issues.

Scheduling

---

### When is the FRM Exam offered?

FRM Exams are offered each year in May, August, and November at testing sites around the world. Seats are available on a first-come, first-served basis. To learn more about how to schedule an Exam, please visit our Exam Logistics page.

Exam Logistics

Program Cost

---

### How much does the FRM Program cost?

The cost of the FRM Program varies based on whether a candidate is registering during the early or standard registration window. Registration also includes one year of complimentary GARP Individual Membership for FRM candidates who have not previously received it from a different GARP program. For a specific pricing breakdown, visit our Fees and Payments page below.

Fees and Payments

Study Materials

---

### What study materials are available?

GARP offers study resources, official practice exams for each exam part, and more to help with preparation. Candidates can access the full curriculum via our comprehensive online GARP Learning platform, complimentary for Part I candidates and available for purchase for Part II candidates. Printed books for both Exam curriculums are also available for purchase. To view all resources, visit our Study Materials page.

Study Materials

Time Commitment

---

### How long does it take to prepare for the FRM Exam?

The FRM Exam is a rigorous evaluation of one's financial risk management expertise, demanding substantial preparation. Candidates typically invest around 240 hours over several months in study time, though this can vary based on individual factors. GARP advises early registration to allow for a comfortable study pace.

## Benefits for Chinese Candidates

The FRM Certification is approved by the Occupational Skill Testing Authority (OSTA) and the Ministry of Human Resources and Social Security (MOHRSS) in China Mainland as a National Vocational Qualification Certificate. More than 25,000 FRM-Certified Professionals are currently employed by every major Chinese financial institution.

Upon successful completion of the program, candidates in China Mainland will receive a certificate acknowledging their achievement and inclusion among a select group of global risk managers. They will also receive a certificate in Chinese and can verify their certification status below to be listed as globally certified risk professionals.

Starting with May 1 registration for November 2025 Exams, GARP will offer its FRM Exam in both Simplified Chinese and American English in Mainland China. This first-time offering will assist in empowering the Chinese-speaking community by providing them with the tools and knowledge needed to excel in the field of financial risk management.

# What is the Chartered Market Technician® (CMT) Program?

CMT Program Guide Register for the CMT Exam Explore the Learning Objectives

## Why Choose the CMT Program?

Choosing the Chartered Market Technician® (CMT) Program is about building a powerful foundation in technical analysis to navigate financial markets with precision and confidence. Recognized globally, the CMT Program delivers cutting-edge insights into behavioral finance, risk management, and quantitative strategies, making it the industry’s highest distinction in technical analysis.

Whether you’re advancing your career in asset management, trading, or investment advising, the CMT curriculum equips you with practical, data-driven tools to interpret market trends and manage risk effectively. As a CMT charterholder, you’ll join a prestigious network of professionals who are shaping the future of finance, backed by CMT Association's commitment to high ethical standards and continual professional development.

Elevate your expertise, deepen your market insights, and distinguish yourself in a competitive field—choose the CMT Program.

## How to become a CMT Charterholder

## 1

#### Pass all three levels of the CMT Exams

The CMT Program consists of three comprehensive exams. To earn the Chartered Market Technician® designation, candidates must pass all three levels, which build technical analysis proficiency, from basic concepts to advanced trading strategies.

## 2

#### Accumulate qualifying professional work experience

Candidates need relevant work experience that contributes directly to financial market analysis or investment decision-making. Experience can be accumulated before, during, or after participating in the CMT Program.

## 3

#### Apply for Professional Membership status

Candidates must join CMT Association as Professional Members, including submitting professional references to verify work and character, to earn their charter.

#### Free access to their charting software for all candidates enrolled in the CMT Program + FREE CMT Level I prep course for registered candidates at CMT Level I

Along with free access to Optuma, the most advanced Technical Analysis application, you can also get access to the CMT Level I prep course candidates are raving about for FREE.

Do note that these offerings are the intellectual property of our partners and provided at their discretion. These are liable to change at any time, without notice. CMT Association will not be liable for partner services.

The Optuma CMT Level I Course provides you with:

- Over 15 hours of detailed video instruction
- Notes for each lesson
- Access to a private forum where you can ask questions to a qualified CMT expert
- Technical Analysis Glossary— all the definitions you need for CMT Level I and CMT Level II
- Indicator List— all the indicators you need to know about
- Knowledge Domains by Chapter— a critical resource in the final weeks before the exam.

You will have everything you need to pass the CMT Level I exam!
